intro:
Creation is the act by which God brought all things — the universe, the world, and life — into being out of nothing (ex nihilo) by the power of his word: "In the beginning, God created the heavens and the earth" (Genesis 1:1); "the universe was created by the word of God, so that what is seen was not made out of things that are visible" (Hebrews 11:3; Psalms 33:6). This establishes the fundamental Creator–creature distinction: God alone is eternal and self-existent; everything else is made, dependent, and accountable to him.

The Genesis account presents creation as ordered, purposeful, and good — God speaks, separates, fills, and pronounces each work good, the whole "very good" (Genesis 1:31). Its climax is humanity, made male and female in God’s image to fill the earth and rule it as his stewards (Genesis 1:26–28), followed by God’s own Sabbath rest (Genesis 2:2–3). Creation is not a rival deity to be worshiped but a gift that points beyond itself to the Giver.

Scripture returns to creation as a ground for worship, wisdom, and humility: "The heavens declare the glory of God" (Psalms 19:1), so that his "eternal power and divine nature… have been clearly perceived" in what he has made, leaving all without excuse (Romans 1:20). God not only made but continually sustains all things, "upholding the universe by the word of his power" (Hebrews 1:3; Acts 17:24–25).

The New Testament names the agent of creation: all things were made through the Son and for him — "without him was not any thing made that was made" (John 1:3; Colossians 1:16–17; Hebrews 1:2). Yet creation now lies under the curse of human sin, groaning for redemption (Romans 8:19–22). In Christ that renewal has begun — "if anyone is in Christ, he is a new creation" (2 Corinthians 5:17) — and will be consummated in "a new heaven and a new earth" (Revelation 21:1; Isaiah 65:17).
